children.name: Pipistrellus
children.description: Pipistrelles — the smallest and most abundant bats in Europe. The common pipistrelle weighs just 3–8 grams but can consume 3,000 midges per night, making it an extraordinarily effective insect controller. Pipistrelles echolocate at characteristic frequencies that allow species identification: the common pipistrelle calls at around 45 kHz, the soprano pipistrelle at 55 kHz. These two species were only distinguished in the 1990s using bat detectors and now known to be genetically and ecologically distinct. They roost in crevices of buildings and trees, with maternity colonies forming in spring.

children.name: Pipistrellus pipistrellus
children.description: Britain's most common bat. Weighs as little as 3 g. A single individual may eat 3,000 insects in a single night. First bat to emerge after dusk, often visible over gardens and along woodland edges.

children.name: Pipistrellus pygmaeus
children.description: Not separated from the common pipistrelle until 1999. Calls at 55 kHz versus 45 kHz for common pipistrelle. Strongly associated with riparian habitats and tends to roost closer to water.

children.name: Pipistrellus nathusii
children.description: One of Europe's great long-distance migrants, with recorded movements exceeding 2,000 km between Baltic breeding grounds and Atlantic wintering areas. Slightly larger than the common pipistrelle, with shaggier fur and a broader wing. Named after the German naturalist Hermann von Nathusius.

children.name: Pipistrellus kuhlii
children.description: A pale-winged pipistrelle with a distinctive white trailing edge to the wing membrane that is visible in flight. Extends from the Mediterranean into the Middle East and across southern Asia to India. Adapts well to urban environments, roosting in buildings and foraging under streetlights.

children.name: Pipistrellus abramus
children.description: The most common bat in Japan, found from Hokkaido to subtropical Ryukyu Islands. Highly commensal with humans, forming large maternity colonies in crevices of buildings. Echolocates at 50–55 kHz; often recorded in cities and agricultural landscapes.

children.name: Pipistrellus rusticus
children.description: A small pipistrelle from sub-Saharan Africa; distinctive reddish-brown fur; roosts in hollow trees.

children.name: Pipistrellus tenuipinnis
children.description: A West African species with unusually narrow wings; forages in dense forest understorey.

children.name: Pipistrellus subflavus
children.description: A small North American bat; tricoloured fur; hibernates singly in caves.

children.name: Pipistrellus alpestris
children.description: A high-altitude bat of the European Alps; recorded at up to 2500 m elevation.

children.name: Pipistrellus stenopterus
children.description: A rare pipistrelle from Southeast Asia; long narrow wings adapted for open-country foraging.

children.name: Plecotus
children.description: Long-eared bats — among the most distinctive of European bats, with ears nearly as long as their body that fold down under the wing membrane when roosting. The exceptionally large ears are an adaptation for detecting the faint rustling sounds of resting insects on leaves — they are 'gleaners' that can pluck moths and beetles off foliage as well as catching them in flight. Long-eared bats are among the most sedentary of European bats, rarely moving more than 10 km from their roost. They are particularly sensitive to loss of old buildings, which provide their preferred maternity roosts.

children.name: Plecotus auritus
children.description: The second most common bat in Britain. Roosts in the apex of old buildings and churches. The enormous ears give them exceptional directional hearing for detecting prey on leaves and bark.

children.name: Plecotus austriacus
children.description: Rare and declining across its European range. In Britain, fewer than 1,000 individuals survive. Has a more southerly and coastal distribution than the brown long-eared bat.

children.name: Plecotus macrobullaris
children.description: Only distinguished from Plecotus auritus in the 1990s using molecular analysis, and restricted to mountain zones from the Alps through the Caucasus to Central Asia. Forages at higher elevations than related species, gleaning prey from rock faces and alpine meadows.

children.name: Plecotus kolombatovici
children.description: A Mediterranean endemic known from karst cave systems of the Adriatic coast, Canary Islands, and North Africa. One of the rarest of the European Plecotus species; described in the 1980s and named after Croatian zoologist Juraj Kolombatović.

children.name: Plecotus ogilviei
children.description: Found in Central Asia and Siberia; extremely long ears for detecting insect rustling.

children.name: Plecotus taivanus
children.description: Endemic to Taiwan; inhabits montane forests above 2000 m; one of the rarest Plecotus species.

children.name: Plecotus sardus
children.description: An endemic long-eared bat of Sardinia; distinguished by genetics from P. auritus.

children.name: Plecotus strepens
children.description: A recently described species from the Alps; exceptionally long ears.

children.name: Plecotus tasmaniensis
children.description: A poorly known species from Tasmania; related to P. auritus.

children.name: Nyctalus
children.description: Noctules — large, fast-flying bats that hunt in open airspace high above the canopy, taking large moths, cockchafers, and even other bats. The common noctule is one of the fastest bats in Europe, reaching 50 km/h and commuting up to 100 km to good foraging sites. Noctules roost almost exclusively in tree holes — old woodpecker cavities and natural splits — and are highly migratory across Europe, tracking insect abundance across seasons. Their calls (typically 20–25 kHz) can be detected easily with bat detectors and are one of the first nocturnal sounds heard after dusk.

children.name: Nyctalus noctula
children.description: Often the first bat of the evening, visible high above treelines before dusk. A long-distance migrant; Scandinavian noctules winter in southern Europe. Hunts moths and beetles detected at long range with low-frequency echolocation.

children.name: Nyctalus leisleri
children.description: The Lesser Noctule, about two-thirds the size of the common noctule but with a similar ecology of open-air hunting. Ireland holds Europe's largest population. Named for German naturalist Johann Philipp Achilles Leisler.

children.name: Nyctalus lasiopterus
children.description: Europe's largest bat, with a wingspan up to 46 cm. Uniquely among European bats, it is known to prey on passerine birds during spring and autumn migration, intercepting them during nocturnal flights. Roosts in old-growth trees; very rare and patchily distributed.

children.name: Nyctalus azoreum
children.description: Endemic to the Azores; the smallest Nyctalus; forages over lakes and pastures; endemic subpopulation on each island.

children.name: Nyctalus plancyi
children.description: Found in China and southeastern Asia; one of the lesser-known noctules.

children.name: Myotis
children.description: The largest bat genus, with over 120 species worldwide. Myotis bats are diverse in their foraging strategies: Daubenton's bat skims the water surface catching emerging insects and even small fish; the whiskered bat hunts through woodland; the greater mouse-eared bat, one of the largest European bats, hunts large beetles on the ground. Most Myotis species roost in caves for winter hibernation, where they form mixed-species colonies. Whitenosesyndrome — caused by the fungus Pseudogymnoascus destructans — has killed millions of hibernating Myotis bats in North America since 2006.

children.name: Myotis daubentonii
children.description: The 'water bat' — a familiar sight skimming rivers and lakes just centimetres above the surface, scooping insects and occasionally small fish with its large hind feet. Roosts in bridges and tunnels near water.

children.name: Myotis mystacinus
children.description: One of the smallest Myotis species. Hunts in woodland edge, gardens, and along hedgerows. Forms maternity roosts in old buildings; one of the commonest bat species found roosting in Swedish churches.

children.name: Myotis attenboroughi
children.description: The only mammal species endemic to Trinidad and Tobago, also recorded from Grenada and Suriname. Named in honour of Sir David Attenborough for his decades of advocacy for the natural world. A small insectivorous bat of Caribbean forests; its precise roosting habits and population size remain poorly studied.

children.name: Myotis lucifugus
children.description: Once the most abundant bat in North America, forming maternity colonies of hundreds of thousands in buildings and caves. Devastated by White-nose Syndrome since 2006, with some populations declining by over 90 per cent. Hibernates in large caves, where infected individuals can spread the lethal fungus rapidly.

children.name: Myotis myotis
children.description: One of Europe's largest bats, with a wingspan up to 45 cm. A specialist ground-forager that uses passive hearing to locate large beetles and crickets walking through short grass, often landing to capture prey. Extinct in Britain but slowly returning via the Channel.

children.name: Myotis blythii
children.description: Closely related to the greater mouse-eared bat and sometimes found roosting alongside it. Distinguished by slightly smaller size and a pale spot on the forehead. Hunts orthopteran insects in dry grasslands and Mediterranean scrubland.

children.name: Myotis nattereri
children.description: A woodland specialist that forages among dense foliage, gleaning prey from leaves and surfaces using very high-frequency echolocation. Has distinctive fringe of stiff hairs along the trailing edge of the tail membrane. Named for Austrian naturalist Johann Natterer.

children.name: Myotis emarginatus
children.description: Identified by a distinctive notch in the outer edge of the ear. A cave-roosting species of Mediterranean and Middle Eastern regions, forming large maternity colonies of thousands in warm roof-spaces and caves. Named for French zoologist Étienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire.

children.name: Myotis septentrionalis
children.description: A forest-interior specialist of eastern and central North America that gleans prey from vegetation. Federally listed as Threatened in the US due to catastrophic declines from White-nose Syndrome; some hibernating populations declined by over 99 per cent. Roosts beneath bark of dead trees in summer.

children.name: Myotis sodalis
children.description: A federally Endangered species that winters in massive hibernating clusters in only a handful of limestone caves in the US Midwest, making it extremely vulnerable. Historically declining due to cave disturbance; now also severely impacted by White-nose Syndrome. Forages along riparian forest edges in summer.

children.name: Myotis grisescens
children.description: Distinguished by uniformly grey fur from base to tip — unlike most bats whose fur is bicoloured. Virtually the entire population of nearly 2 million individuals hibernates in just nine caves in the US. Federally listed as Endangered but recovering due to cave protection; also impacted by White-nose Syndrome.

children.name: Myotis evotis
children.description: One of the longest-eared bats in North America, using its large ears for passive detection of prey movements. Forages in open coniferous forests of western North America, gleaning insects from foliage and bark. Roosts in crevices of rock outcrops and tree cavities.

children.name: Myotis velifer
children.description: Forms enormous cave-roosting colonies — some exceeding 100,000 individuals — in the semi-arid regions of the American Southwest and Mexico. One of the most abundant bat species in its range. Emerges at dusk in dense, swirling columns that are visible from kilometres away.

children.name: Myotis vivesi
children.description: One of the world's only piscivorous bats, using echolocation to detect ripples made by fish near the sea surface around islands in the Gulf of California. Has enormously enlarged hind feet with robust, recurved claws for gaffing fish from the water. Restricted to rocky islets and listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Myotis adversus
children.description: A water-foraging species of Southeast Asia and Australasia that trawls for fish and aquatic invertebrates using its disproportionately large hind feet. Roosts in caves and culverts close to water. Widely distributed from southern China to northern Australia.

children.name: Myotis bechsteinii
children.description: A rare woodland bat; named after Johann Bechstein; roosts in tree cavities and forages in closed forest; severely declined across Europe.

children.name: Myotis dasycneme
children.description: A large Myotis with hairy feet adapted for trawling insects from water surfaces.

children.name: Myotis brandtii
children.description: Separated from the whiskered bat in 1970; one of the longest-lived mammals for its size.

children.name: Eptesicus
children.description: Serotines — large, slow-flying bats that hunt in open woodland and parkland, taking large moths and beetles. The serotine is one of the largest British bats, appearing similar to a noctule but with a distinctly longer, broader wing tip allowing slower, more manoeuvrable flight. They roost exclusively in buildings — often in the roofspace or within cavity walls — and rarely use caves. The big brown bat (Eptesicus fuscus) is the most widespread bat in North America and the species most frequently found roosting in houses there.

children.name: Eptesicus serotinus
children.description: One of the largest bats in Europe; wingspan up to 38 cm. Has a distinctive low, slow flight. Roosts only in buildings; a maternity roost in a house roof may persist for decades or centuries.

children.name: Eptesicus fuscus
children.description: The most frequently encountered bat in North America, thriving in cities, suburbs, and farmland. A hardy hibernator that can survive temperatures near freezing for months without food. Consumes enormous quantities of agricultural pest moths and beetles, with colonies estimated to save billions of dollars in pest control annually.

children.name: Eptesicus nilssonii
children.description: The northernmost bat in the world, breeding above the Arctic Circle in Scandinavia and Russia. Has a relatively slow metabolic rate for its size, an adaptation to the short, insect-rich boreal summer. Named for Swedish naturalist Sven Nilsson.

children.name: Eptesicus bottae
children.description: Found in the Middle East and Central Asia; pale sandy fur matches desert habitats.

children.name: Eptesicus hottentotus
children.description: A southern African species with an exceptionally long tail for its body.

children.name: Eptesicus pumilus
children.description: A small serotine from Timor and surrounding islands; insectivorous and crepuscular.

children.name: Eptesicus brasiliensis
children.description: A Neotropical serotine; the southernmost Eptesicus in the Americas.

children.name: Eptesicus gobiensis
children.description: A serotine adapted to the harsh desert climate of the Gobi and Mongolian steppes.

children.name: Lasiurus
children.description: Hairy-tailed bats — a New World genus characterised by dense fur extending onto the tail membrane and highly migratory behaviour. The hoary bat is the most wide-ranging land mammal in the Americas, with populations in Hawaii representing a 1,000 km ocean crossing. Lasiurus species are largely solitary tree-roosting bats that camouflage among foliage; females can give birth to litters of up to four young — unusual for bats. Several species have suffered severe mortality at wind turbines, raising concern about population viability.

children.name: Lasiurus borealis
children.description: One of the most strikingly coloured bats; males are brick-red, females chestnut-brown, with a white shoulder patch. A solitary tree-roosting migrant that blends with dead leaves in autumn. One of the bat species most killed by wind turbines during migration.

children.name: Lasiurus cinereus
children.description: North America's largest migratory bat, with frost-tipped fur that provides perfect leaf litter camouflage. Breeds as far north as Canada and winters in Mexico and Central America. The sole land mammal native to Hawaii, where an isolated population diverged into the Hawaiian hoary bat.

children.name: Lasiurus seminolus
children.description: A mahogany-furred bat of the pine forests and Spanish moss of the southeastern United States. Year-round resident that does not migrate, roosting in clumps of Spanish moss (Tillandsia usneoides). Closely related to the eastern red bat but darker and non-migratory.

children.name: Lasiurus ega
children.description: A yellow-furred bat of arid and semi-arid tropical regions from southern Texas and Baja California through South America to Argentina. Roosts in palm fronds and yucca leaves. Has broad, long wings suited for fast, direct flight over open terrain.

children.name: Lasiurus blaisdellii
children.description: Formerly considered a subspecies of L. borealis; distinguished by genetic markers.

children.name: Lasiurus castaneus
children.description: A rare highland species from Central America to Colombia; chestnut-coloured fur.

children.name: Lasiurus egregius
children.description: The only Lasiurus in the Asian tropics; found only on Sulawesi; poorly known.

children.name: Perimyotis
children.description: A monotypic North American genus containing only the tricolored bat, formerly placed in Pipistrellus. Distinctive for its tri-banded fur — dark brown at the base, pale in the middle, dark at the tip — and its low echolocation frequencies. The genus has been placed in its own lineage based on genetic data, representing a deep divergence within vespertilionids.

children.name: Perimyotis subflavus
children.description: A small, slow-flying bat of forest edges in eastern North America, identifiable by its diagnostic tricoloured fur and pinkish-brown forearms. Formerly called the Eastern Pipistrelle. Severely impacted by White-nose Syndrome; listed as Endangered and under review for federal listing in the US.

children.name: Lasionycteris
children.description: A monotypic North American genus containing only the silver-haired bat. Intermediate in size and ecology between the small pipistrelles and large noctules, the silver-haired bat is a long-distance migrant that forages in slow, fluttering flight along forest edges and over water. It represents an isolated lineage with no close living relatives within the family.

children.name: Lasionycteris noctivagans
children.description: Unmistakable with black fur tipped in silver-white, giving it a frosted appearance. One of the most common bats to be found migrating through cities; frequently reported from upper floors of tall buildings. Roosts in tree cavities and under bark, and rarely enters man-made structures.

children.name: Antrozous
children.description: A monotypic North American genus containing only the pallid bat. Among the most unusual vespertilionids, it has extremely large ears and forages primarily on the ground, using passive hearing alone to locate large ground-dwelling arthropods, scorpions, and even small lizards without any echolocation. It is immune to the venom of the scorpions it consumes. Pallid bats are highly social, roosting in large colonies in rock crevices of arid western landscapes.

children.name: Antrozous pallidus
children.description: A pale, large-eared bat of western North American deserts that hunts on or near the ground using passive hearing, specialising in scorpions, beetles, and crickets. It is immune to Arizona bark scorpion venom. Important pollinator of the saguaro and cardon cacti; its face is often dusted with pollen.

children.name: Corynorhinus
children.description: Lump-nosed bats — a small North American genus of slow-flying, large-eared gleaning bats. Like the Old World Plecotus, they use their enormous ears to detect the sounds of resting prey. Rafinesque's big-eared bat is closely associated with old-growth bottomland hardwood forest and is considered an indicator of forest health. The Ozark big-eared bat is one of the rarest mammals in North America.

children.name: Corynorhinus rafinesquii
children.description: A large-eared bat of the southeastern US that roosts in hollow trees and old buildings in bottomland hardwood forests. Named for the polymath naturalist Constantine Samuel Rafinesque-Schmaltz, one of the most prolific describers of North American species. Listed as a Species of Greatest Conservation Need in most states.

children.name: Corynorhinus townsendii
children.description: Has the largest ears of any bat in western North America, measuring up to 4 cm long and joined at the base across the forehead. Roosts in caves and mine tunnels, forming clusters sensitive to disturbance. Two subspecies (the Virginia and Ozark big-eared bats) are federally listed as Endangered. Named for explorer John Kirk Townsend.

children.name: Idionycteris
children.description: A monotypic genus restricted to montane forests of the American Southwest and Mexico. Allen's big-eared bat is characterised by unusually long ears with a distinctive basal lobe, and by foraging at forest edges in montane conifer forest — a habitat not typically used by other large-eared vespertilionids in its range. It is poorly studied and its population status is uncertain.

children.name: Idionycteris phyllotis
children.description: A montane bat of pine-oak and conifer forests in the southwestern US and Mexico, recognisable by extremely long ears with a prominent basal lobe. Gleans moths and other insects from foliage at night. Named for naturalist Joel Asaph Allen, who described many North American bats and mammals.

children.name: Barbastella
children.description: Barbastelles — distinctive bats with short, broad faces and unusual nostrils that point upward and forward rather than forward, related to specialised quiet echolocation that allows them to approach acoustically camouflaged moths without detection. The western barbastelle hunts tiger moths that have evolved to detect and avoid bat echolocation — the bat counters by emitting calls 10–100 times quieter than other species, making them effectively 'whispering' bats. Barbastelles roost behind loose bark and in tree cavities.

children.name: Barbastella barbastellus
children.description: A cryptic specialist of old-growth woodland that uses uniquely quiet echolocation to ambush tiger moths that would otherwise detect and evade it. One of Europe's rarest and most poorly known bats; strongly associated with veteran trees with loose bark for roosting.

children.name: Barbastella leucomelas
children.description: The eastern counterpart to the western barbastelle, found from the Caucasus and Middle East east through Central Asia to Japan. Often associated with rocky desert canyons and forest edges. Has been recorded at elevations above 3,000 m in Central Asia.

children.name: Hypsugo
children.description: Small cave-dwelling pipistrelle-like bats of Africa and Asia, formerly lumped in Pipistrellus but now recognised as a distinct genus based on molecular phylogenies. They occupy a broad range of habitats from Mediterranean forests to tropical savanna. The European species Hypsugo savii is one of the most altitude-tolerant bats in the world, recorded roosting in crevices above 2,000 m in the Alps.

children.name: Hypsugo savii
children.description: A high-altitude bat of Mediterranean mountains that roosts in rock crevices and stone buildings, emerging before dusk to hunt in open aerial space. Among European bats the most tolerant of cold at elevation. Named for Italian naturalist Paolo Savi.

children.name: Otonycteris
children.description: A monotypic genus of the arid zone, containing the desert long-eared bat. Like the pallid bat of North America, it has evolved enormous ears as an adaptation to passive prey detection in hot deserts, where echolocation alone may be inefficient. It consumes scorpions regularly and appears to be physiologically resistant to their venom — a parallel adaptation to that seen in Antrozous pallidus.

children.name: Otonycteris hemprichii
children.description: A pale, large-eared bat of North African and Middle Eastern deserts, structurally convergent with the pallid bat. Forages low to the ground in arid landscapes, hunting scorpions and large arthropods using passive hearing. Named for German naturalist Wilhelm Hemprich.

children.name: Vespertilio
children.description: The bicoloured bat — the nominate genus of the family Vespertilionidae, giving the family its name. A fast, open-air hunter with a characteristic bicoloured pattern — dark fur above and creamy-white below — that appears in both European species. Unlike most vespertilionids, Vespertilio is strongly associated with open water bodies, often seen hawking insects over rivers and lakes.

children.name: Vespertilio murinus
children.description: A large, fast-flying bat with striking bicoloured fur — dark brown above, white below — and white-frosted wing membranes. Males produce a distinctive metallic social call audible to humans. A long-distance migrant; Central Asian populations winter in South Asia.

children.name: Vespertilio sinensis
children.description: The eastern counterpart to the parti-coloured bat, distributed across East Asia from China through Korea to Japan. Very similar in appearance to Vespertilio murinus but separated by range and genetic data. Forages over rivers and wetlands, emerging early in the evening.

children.name: Scotophilus
children.description: House bats — African and Asian bats that are among the most commensal of all bat species, roosting almost entirely in buildings and thatch-roofed houses. Several species are among the most common bats in sub-Saharan Africa. They are large, robust bats with yellowish-brown or grey coats, and are important insect controllers in agricultural landscapes, particularly around rice fields.

children.name: Scotophilus kuhlii
children.description: The most widespread house bat of tropical Asia, roosting in colonies in roof spaces of buildings from Pakistan to the Philippines. Strongly yellowish-brown below. Emerges shortly after dusk to forage over open ground and water, taking a wide variety of flying insects.

children.name: Scotophilus dinganii
children.description: The common house bat of sub-Saharan Africa, roosting in groups under the eaves and roofing sheets of buildings across the continent. Named after the Zulu king Dingane. Plays an important role in controlling mosquitoes and agricultural pest insects.

children.name: Scotophilus leucugus
children.description: A large house bat from southern Africa; white belly sharply demarcated from brown back.

children.name: Scotophilus nux
children.description: A Central African rainforest species; chestnut-brown fur; roosts in dead palm fronds.

children.name: Nycticeius
children.description: Evening bats — a small New World genus closely related to Eptesicus. The evening bat is one of the most abundant bats in the southeastern United States, forming large maternity colonies in hollow trees. They are notably fast colonisers of disturbed landscapes and are among the most agriculturally important bats in cotton-growing regions, where they consume vast numbers of corn earworm moths.

children.name: Nycticeius humeralis
children.description: A small, dark brown bat of the eastern and central United States that forms maternity colonies of hundreds to thousands in hollow trees and rarely in buildings. Distinct from pipistrelles by its single upper incisor on each side. A voracious predator of corn earworm moths with major economic value.

children.name: Kerivoula
children.description: Painted bats and woolly bats — among the most beautiful of all bats, with spectacular orange-and-black colouration in some species. Kerivoula bats are forest specialists of Africa and Asia with broad, rounded wings suited to manoeuvring in dense vegetation. They echolocate at very high frequencies (above 100 kHz in some species) for highly precise targeting of prey in cluttered forest understorey.

children.name: Kerivoula picta
children.description: One of the most visually striking mammals in the world, with brilliant orange fur contrasting with jet-black wing membranes and ears. Roosts in clusters that remarkably mimic dried seed pods or dead leaves. Widely distributed in South and Southeast Asian forests.

children.name: Kerivoula hardwickii
children.description: A small, woolly-furred bat of South and Southeast Asia. Remarkably, individuals have been found roosting inside the pitcher traps of Nepenthes rafflesiana pitcher plants, where they roost in return for contributing nitrogen-rich droppings that fertilise the plant — a striking mutualism. Named for Major-General Thomas Hardwicke.

children.name: Kerivoula papillosa
children.description: A Southeast Asian species with fine papillae on its muzzle; roosts in banana leaves.

children.name: Kerivoula pellucida
children.description: Has strikingly translucent wings with visible veins; inhabits lowland forests of Borneo.

children.name: Murina
children.description: Tube-nosed bats — Asian bats with distinctive tubular nostrils that project outward from the face, possibly functioning to direct echolocation calls. Murina species are forest-interior bats that forage in dense understorey, often among bamboo or low vegetation. The genus has expanded dramatically in recent decades with molecular sampling; over a dozen new species have been described since 2000.

children.name: Murina cyclotis
children.description: A small tube-nosed bat widely distributed from India through Southeast Asia to China, characterised by rounded ears and projecting tubular nostrils. Forages in dense forest understorey at low altitude. Often roosts in the furled leaves of banana and heliconia plants.

children.name: Murina hilgendorfi
children.description: A Japanese species with distinctive tubelike nostrils; hibernates in limestone caves.

children.name: Murina aurata

children.name: Chalinolobus
children.description: Wattled bats — an Australasian and sub-Saharan African genus with a prominent fleshy lobe at the corner of the mouth. Chalinolobus species are fast, open-air hunters that forage over open woodland and grassland. The chocolate wattled bat (C. morio) is one of the most common bats in southern Australia, while Pekapeka (C. tuberculatus), the long-tailed bat of New Zealand, is a threatened endemic with few remaining cave roost sites.

children.name: Chalinolobus gouldii
children.description: The most widespread bat in Australia, found in every mainland state and territory. A fast, high-flying species that forages over open woodland and pastoral land. Roosts in tree hollows, buildings, and occasionally in other bats' roosts. Named for ornithologist and naturalist John Gould.

children.name: Chalinolobus tuberculatus
children.description: One of only two surviving bat species endemic to New Zealand (Pekapeka in Māori). Classified as Threatened; populations have declined severely due to predation by introduced stoats, rats, and possums. Roosts in large tree hollow colonies; forages over forest streams.

children.name: Chalinolobus morio
children.description: A common Australian bat; known for its glossy chocolate-brown fur; roosts in tree hollows.

children.name: Chalinolobus nigrogrieus
children.description: Found in eastern Australia; greyish fur with white-tipped hairs giving a frosted appearance.

children.name: Vespadelus
children.description: Forest bats — a genus of small, dark-furred bats endemic to Australia and New Guinea, formerly placed in Eptesicus. They are among the smallest bats in Australia and fill the pipistrelle ecological niche there. Many species are specialised cave-roosters in addition to using tree hollows, and several species are known from only one or two localities.

children.name: Vespadelus vulturnus
children.description: The smallest bat in Australia, weighing just 3–4.5 g, and one of the smallest mammals on the continent. Forages in slow, highly manoeuvrable flight in dense eucalypt forest understorey. Despite its small size, its echolocation reaches peak frequencies of up to 77 kHz.

children.name: Vespadelus darlingtoni
children.description: The largest of the Australian forest bats. Found in temperate and wet sclerophyll forests of southeastern Australia and Tasmania, where it roosts behind bark and in tree hollows. Named for American zoologist Philip Darlington. Forages at canopy level for moths and beetles.

children.name: Scotomanes
children.description: A monotypic Asian genus containing the harlequin bat, one of the most distinctive bats in Asia. With spotted white patches on a dark brown background, it is thought to roost in dead leaves or on bark where its colouration provides camouflage. It is the largest species in the tribe Vespertilionini and occupies humid foothill and montane forests.

children.name: Scotomanes ornatus
children.description: A large, vividly patterned bat of South and Southeast Asian foothill forests, with conspicuous white spots on a dark brown body. Roosts singly in tree hollows and forages at forest edges. Its unusual colouration may serve as disruptive camouflage against patterned bark and leaf litter.

children.name: Arielulus
children.description: Sprite bats — a genus of small, brightly coloured bats of South and Southeast Asia, formerly placed in Pipistrellus. Several species have striking golden or orange facial colouring and iridescent fur. They occupy forested hill and montane zones and are poorly studied; several species were described only in the 21st century.

children.name: Arielulus aureocollaris
children.description: A small, spectacularly marked bat with a vivid golden-yellow collar contrasting with dark brown fur on the back and head. Known from tropical highland forests of Thailand and southern China. Described in 2006 and among the more recently named members of its genus.

children.name: Falsistrellus
children.description: False pipistrelles — a small Australasian genus of medium-sized bats, larger and more robustly built than true pipistrelles. Found in the mature eucalypt forests of Australia and in forests of New Guinea and the Philippines. Rely heavily on large tree hollows for roosting and are sensitive to logging that removes old-growth trees.

children.name: Falsistrellus tasmaniensis
children.description: A large, fast-flying bat of tall eucalypt forests in southeastern Australia and Tasmania. Roosts in groups in large tree hollows of old-growth trees. Among the faster Australasian bats, hunting moths in open aerial space above the forest canopy.

children.name: Tadarida
children.description: Fast-flying bats with a long tail extending beyond the tail membrane; among the fastest mammals in level flight.

children.name: Tadarida teniotis
children.description: The only free-tailed bat in Europe; echolocation audible to some humans as a low ticking sound.

children.name: Tadarida brasiliensis
children.description: Forms the largest known bat colonies; Bracken Cave houses 20 million individuals.

children.name: Tadarida australis
children.description: Australia's largest insectivorous bat; white stripes on shoulders; fast flier reaching 60 km/h.

children.description: Found in eastern Australia and New Guinea; roosts in caves and mines in small colonies.
